paragraphs.body: The U: The Catered Restaurant of Your Hopes and Dreams

Appetizers $1-Listen to the Crowd Do whatever you want to do. Did you enjoy high school? Then the next best step would be college. Go to the college of your choice. Do you like urban, or rural? Large campus size or small? Party School or Academics? The choice is yours, but your teachers and advisor and siblings and parents and friends and relatives and even strangers that have no clue who you are will offer you unwarranted advice.

$2-Listen to your Head Do whatever you want to do. Did you hate high school? Then maybe trade school is for you. You can join your friends in high school who didn’t take it seriously and end up doing a blue-collar job for the rest of your life. But hey, you won’t be drowning in debt and probably end up making more money than many people heading off the University. $3-Listen to your Heart Do whatever you want to do. Did you hate school so much you never want to set foot in any again? Well, you can enter the labor force immediately! Join many Americans working their favorite service jobs and start collecting checks immediately.

Entrees $20-Engineering Wouldn’t this be a good choice. Majority of your friends are in different types of engineering. After all, The University of Pittsburgh is a fantastic school for this program. While it is difficult, the grueling work will pay off when you graduate, by paying well. You could join the swarms of Pitt students in Calculus, Physics, and all the other courses that you have no interest in and will probably fail at some point. $20-Biological and Biomedical Sciences

Another fantastic option. Join what seems like your entire floor of your dorm in another popular choice. This choice requires a palette for Biology, Chemistry, and all those evil classes you hated in high school. You will probably have to stay in school for a very long time, accumulate massive amounts of debt, and most likely not enjoy your occupation. $15-Business, Management, or Marketing Classic major option. Easier than the previous options. Could you offer a more flexible schedule and less grueling workload. You could enjoy your life more and only have to worry till after graduation to find a boring job, (if you can find one), and then enjoy cubical office life for the rest of your days.

annotations.text: choice. This choice requires a palette for Biology, Chemistry, and all those evil classes you hated in high school. You will probably have to stay in school for a very long time, accumulate massive amounts of debt, and most likely not enjoy your occupation.
annotations.comment: I really appreciate moments like this when you really cut to the chase. I think, very often, we try to sugar coat the difficulty and debt associated with particular courses of study, and someone considering these fields would really benefit by factoring this into the equation.

paragraphs.body: $10-Psychology This would be a lot of fun. You’ve already enrolled in an Intro to Psychology course, the professor is nice and you’re having a lot of fun. The only thing to worry about is what to do once you graduate. $10-Political Science/International Relations Follow in the footsteps of your sister, Aunt and Mother. You could take numerous classes that interest you, and help you make sense of the world around you. Try and find a career that could fulfill you. Key word is try, harder and harder to do nowadays. Try not to think about how both your aunt and mother work in fields completely unrelated to this major, or how your sister lost her job. Follow your heart.

Deserts $4-What you Want Keep living the same life. Stay comfortable. Stay on the phone. Talk to the same people. Ignore things you don’t want to do. Remanence about things you use to do, about trips you used to take. Fantasize about what you could do, what you could accomplish. $5- What you Need Grow up. Workout. Go outside. Meet new people. Talk to old friends. Read new books. Read different types of books. Watch unique videos. Grow your mindset. Travel to new places. Try new foods. Be willing to be uncomfortable. 0$- What you Dream Think of your favorite options on the menu. What resonates with you. Think of yourself 20 years from now. If you do the same things, repeat the same habits, continue your current life. Will you be Happy? Will you be fulfilled? Will you have regrets? What are they? What should you have done differently? If you were to disappear right now, what did you wish you did?

annotations.comment: Hello, The central focusing idea of this piece is clearly a consideration of what a young person’s choices are for future study and occupation. The big ideas that bolster this notion are advice one receives, research into occupations, and how to integrate the research and advice into a single beneficial decision. This format really holds up nicely, and I love the way you’re integrating these ideas into this menu format. The phrase that stand out most to me is “Do whatever you want to do” which you repeat a few times in the appetizers section of the menu. It is a great piece of advice, but is just one of many pieces that go into the puzzle of choosing your own future. Also, what if I don’t know what I want to do? Well, that’s what the entrees are for. That’s what the other advice and encouragement is for. The thing that I admire most about this piece of writing is the way you allow the format to guide the repetition and variation of thoughts and phrases. Like a restaurant, which is limited by the ingredients and machinery they have available to them, we are all limited in our choices for the future based on advice, experiences, desires, geography, and some other key factors in how we decide what we are to do with life. On the one hand, the audience for this piece seems to be a younger version of yourself, perhaps this is advice that you had to absorb and consider before you matriculated to Pitt. On the other hand, this piece could have a much broader audience of high school aged people who are considering what to do with their future. Truly, for a lot of such students, a piece of writing such as this could be much more enjoyable and more palatable than brochure after brochure of information about programs and careers. The main thing that I learned from reading this essay is how a form such as this hermit crab essay can, in fact, make such information much more palatable. We’ve all had the experience of ordering food from a restaurant, which means we have to decide what we’re hungry for, how much we need to spend, what would suit our health best. This format has shown me that choosing a future is very similar to choosing a meal—there are a lot of factors that go into such a decision. At any rate, this essay was a pleasure to read, and your voice brought your ideas through in a fascinating way. Thanks,
